title: Vegetable — November 2020 family grocery list
html:
TL;DR

  • 17yo Yahir captures a 30-item household grocery list mid-COVID — a bilingual Mexican-American pantry portrait: serrano, Tapatio, chile cans, tortillas, La Felix, yakukts (Yakult), seaweeds, lentils, salmon, dijon, almonds
  • The list is ungrouped and ungrammatical (typos: "habero", "yakukts", "bread saves") — pure capture mode, the working as scratch
  • Pre-vegetarian-era artifact: ham, chicken-noodle soup, tuna, salmon all present — Yahir's "drive to continue vegetarian" questioning starts ~9 months later
  • Pantry composition documents the Gonzalez Vargas household's food identity: Mexican staples (serrano, chile, tapatio, tortillas) cohabiting with Pacific-Northwest-American (almonds, dijon, salmon, ice cream)
  • One of the densest "this is what was actually in the house" artifacts in the corpus — a counterweight to the abstract aspiration documents from the same week

Notes

Grocery lists are usually noise, but this one is anthropology. The pantry is the thing — what the yahir family eats reads as immigrant-second-generation Pacific Northwest: Mexican-staple base (serrano, Tapatio, chile cans, tortillas, La Felix) with the small town Oregon additions (Dijon, almonds, pecans, salmon, dijon mustard, sour cream, cereal). Yakult hints at the Asian-grocery convenience layer that small-town Oregon was getting in 2020.

Co-located with the scholarship essay todo from 12 days later — same notebook, same period, same operating system: capture everything, sort never. The scholarship-application Yahir and the grocery-list Yahir are the same kid. That's the thesis.

The pre-vegetarian-era status of this note is the most useful retro-data — it dates the food-identity transition to mid-2021, between this list (Nov 2020, omnivore) and the July 2021 "what is your drive to continue vegetarian" question.

Connections
